heroText: Porto-Novo is the capital and second-largest city of Benin.  The commune covers an area of 52 square kilometres (20 sq mi) and as of 2002 had a population of 223,552 people.

highlights.name: Porto-Novo
highlights.text: Porto-Novo is the capital and second-largest city of Benin.

sources.label: Wikipedia: Porto-Novo
sources.usedFor: city description and coordinates where available

history.text: In 1861, the British, who were active in nearby Nigeria, bombarded the city, which caused the Kingdom of Porto-Novo to accept the French offer of protection in 1863.
In 1883, Porto-Novo was incorporated into the French "colony of Dahomey and its dependencies" and in 1900, it became Dahomey's capital city.
From 1908, the king held the title of Chef supérieur.
The kings of Porto-Novo continued to rule in the city, both officially and unofficially, until the death of the last king, Alohinto Gbeffa, in 1976.

history.label: Growth
history.text: It remains untranslated in French, the national language of Benin.

history.label: Modern city
history.text: Porto-Novo was once a tributary of the Yoruba Oyo Empire, which had offered it protection from the neighbouring Fon, who were expanding their influence and power in the region.

history.label: Today
history.text: Today, the Yoruba community in Porto-Novo remains one of the two original ethnic groups in the city.

history.label: Civic role
history.text: The city was originally called Ajashe (Àjàṣẹ́ in Yoruba orthography) by the Yorubas, and Hogbonu by the Gun.

history.label: Regional role
history.text: The neighbouring Kingdom of Dahomey objected to French involvement in the region and war broke out between the two states.

history.label: Regional role
history.text: Unlike the city's earlier Gun migrants, however, the French sought to impose their language in all spheres of life and completely stamp out the use and proliferation of indigenous languages.

history.label: Regional role
history.text: Many Afro-Brazilians settled in Porto-Novo following their return to Africa after emancipation in Brazil.

history.label: Regional role
history.text: Brazilian architecture and foods are important to the city's cultural life.

history.label: Regional role
history.text: Under French colonial rule, flight across the new border to British-ruled Nigeria in order to avoid harsh taxation, military service and forced labour was common.

history.label: Regional role
history.text: A combination of the aforementioned facts, coupled with the fact that the city itself lies within the sphere of Nigerian socioeconomic influence, has given Porto-Novo's residents a preference for some measure of bi-nationality or.
